content: Many developers initially misunderstand the wasm-pack build pipeline by thinking it simply compiles Rust to WebAssembly and outputs a static binary, which leads them to believe they can directly drop that output into any bundler without further configuration. This is a dangerous misconception because wasm-pack does not produce a final runnable module for the browser or Node.js; instead, it generates an intermediate artifact called the shared library, typically named target/wasm-lib.wasm, and simultaneously creates a JavaScript glue layer in the src/lib.rs file that exports types and functions. The real magic happens when you run npm pack, which bundles this wasm binary along with the generated glue code into a single tarball, allowing Node.js environments to resolve imports using the WebAssembly API while browsers must still load the module dynamically via the Fetch or ImportScripts APIs. A common pitfall arises when developers try to use the bundled output in a browser without understanding that the shared library lacks a memory boundary by default; wasm-pack writes this flag to .wasm.toml, but if you manually override it or misconfigure your target, the glue code will panic because it attempts to access memory outside the shared library. Another subtle issue involves integrating with modern bundlers like Vite or webpack; while wasm-pack can generate a package.json with proper exports fields and a main entry point for Node.js, these files are not automatically recognized by browsers as valid module loaders unless you explicitly configure your build tool to treat the bundled tarball content as a WebAssembly module. When integrating into a project using Vite, you must ensure that your index.html loads the script tag pointing directly to the compiled JavaScript file, not the raw wasm binary, and that your Rust code correctly implements the traits required by the glue layer such as std::os::raw::c_void for memory management. Developers often forget that wasm-pack uses a specific directory structure where the target/wasm-lib.wasm is merely one piece of the puzzle; the actual executable file for Node.js might be named index.mjs or similar depending on your configuration, and this requires careful inspection of the generated package.json to ensure the module type is set correctly. Furthermore, when dealing with complex Rust types like Arc<Mutex<T>> or Box<dyn Trait>, wasm-pack's glue generation relies on precise naming conventions in the Rust code; if you expose a function without the #[wasm_bindgen] attribute or misspell an export name, the generated JavaScript will fail to bind correctly at runtime. The most critical insight is that the build pipeline is not just about compilation but also about metadata propagation; wasm-pack reads your Cargo.toml for dependencies and propagates them into package.json so that npm install can resolve transitive Rust crates like serde or tokio without requiring manual dependency mapping in JavaScript. Finally, when targeting a bundler environment specifically, you must remember that the output format depends on whether you are building for Node.js or WebAssembly; for Node.js, wasm-pack bundles everything needed to load and execute the module into one file, whereas for WebAssembly it creates separate files for the binary and the glue code, meaning your integration strategy must diverge based on the target platform. This distinction explains why a single npm pack command can serve both environments but requires different deployment logic: browsers need dynamic loading via script tags while Node.js needs static resolution of the file path in require or import statements.
